figures.caption: Fig. 9: Linear fits between DCI ground-truth final latent errors δ0 (128 test samples; 20 schedules per K∈{2, 6, 10, 14, 18}) and the scoring function S↑(Z). Upcasting gains ∆↑ t are computed on MJHQ with m ∈ {64, 128, 256, 512} calibration samples.

Fig. 10: Linear fits between COCO ground-truth final latent errors δ0 (128 test samples; 20 schedules per K∈{2, 6, 10, 14, 18}) and the scoring function S↑(Z). Upcasting gains ∆↑ t are computed on MJHQ with m ∈ {64, 128, 256, 512} calibration samples.

Table 1: Comparison across different models and datasets. The reference point for similarity evaluation are the images generated by the unquantized (16 bits) model. To compare models with similar speedups, we scale the number of timesteps depending on the data type.

Fig. 8: Linear fits between MJHQ ground-truth final latent errors δ0 (128 test samples; 20 schedules per K∈{2, 6, 10, 14, 18}) and the scoring function S↑(Z). Upcasting gains ∆↑ t are computed on MJHQ with m ∈ {64, 128, 256, 512} calibration samples.

Fig. 7: Final latent error δ0 reduction for single-timestep upcasting, with the upcasting gain ∆↑ t computed on 128 samples of the MJHQ, DCI and COCO datasets respectively (Figure 7a) and for 64, 128, 256 and 512 samples from the MJHQ dataset (Figure 7b) on the Sana model.
